chapter_summary:
Large Low Shear Velocity Provinces (LLSVPs) are continent-sized regions in the lowermost mantle beneath Africa and the Pacific where seismic shear wave velocities are reduced by 2-5% relative to the global average. Whether these anomalies arise from purely thermal effects (elevated temperature reducing elastic moduli) or from thermochemical heterogeneity (iron enrichment or distinct bulk composition) remains one of the central debates in deep Earth geophysics. The LLSVPCalculator in this codebase addresses this question by computing the temperature anomaly (DeltaT) required to produce a given dln(Vs) for a mineral at lower-mantle pressure using the Mie-Gruneisen EOS, and by comparing elastic properties of reference versus chemically anomalous assemblages (e.g., Mg-perovskite vs Fe-perovskite) through the CompareAssemblages method. Typical results show that a purely thermal origin for a -2% dVs anomaly in bridgmanite at 120 GPa requires a temperature excess of several hundred kelvin, which approaches or exceeds plausible geothermal variations, motivating the thermochemical hypothesis.

Ultra-Low Velocity Zones (ULVZs) are thin (5-40 km thick) patches at the core-mantle boundary (CMB) exhibiting extreme velocity reductions of up to -10% in Vp and -30% in Vs. The leading hypothesis attributes these anomalies to partial melting of mantle material at the CMB, where temperatures may exceed the solidus. The ULVZCalculator models this by computing elastic properties of a solid-melt mixture using the Reuss (lower) bound for the bulk modulus and a linear Voigt average for the shear modulus (with G_melt = 0 for liquid). The melt equation of state is parameterized as a third-order Birch-Murnaghan EOS with default silicate melt parameters (rho0 = 3.0 g/cm3, K0 = 30 GPa, K' = 6). Because the melt has zero shear modulus, even a modest melt fraction of 5-10% produces dramatic Vs reductions exceeding 5%, while Vp reductions are more moderate since the melt retains finite bulk modulus.

The connection between LLSVPs and ULVZs is significant: ULVZs are preferentially found at the margins of LLSVPs, suggesting that the chemically anomalous material comprising LLSVPs may have a lower solidus than ambient mantle, promoting partial melting at their edges where hot upwelling plumes are generated. Iron enrichment plays a dual role -- it reduces seismic velocities (lowering Vs more than Vp) and increases density, potentially stabilizing anomalous material against convective entrainment. The Fe-perovskite endmember in the SLB2011 database exhibits both higher density and lower shear velocity than Mg-perovskite at the same conditions, consistent with observations that LLSVPs have sharp lateral boundaries (indicative of chemical rather than purely thermal heterogeneity).

Computationally, the LLSVPCalculator uses bisection over a DeltaT range of 0-2000 K to find the temperature anomaly matching a target dln(Vs), evaluating the Mie-Gruneisen EOS at each trial temperature. The ULVZCalculator solves the melt density via bisection on the BM3 equation of state, then computes mixture properties using effective medium theory. Both calculators depend on the MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer for computing high-pressure, high-temperature mineral properties and accept SLB2011 endmember parameters as input.
prerequisite_knowledge: Mie-Gruneisen equation of state and its numerical solution (Chapter 6)
Elastic moduli and seismic velocity computation at high P-T (Chapter 4)
SLB2011 mineral database: bridgmanite (mpv, fpv) and ferropericlase (pe, wu) endmembers (Chapter 7)
Elastic mixing models: Reuss, Voigt, and Hashin-Shtrikman bounds (Chapter 10)
Birch-Murnaghan equation of state for isothermal compression
Seismology fundamentals: tomographic imaging, shear and compressional wave velocities, PREM (Chapter 15)
Post-perovskite transition and D'' layer structure (Chapter 14)
Thermodynamics of melting: solidus, liquidus, and partial melt

key_equations.name: Logarithmic shear velocity anomaly from thermal perturbation

variables.\\delta \\ln V_s: Fractional change in shear velocity (dimensionless, e.g., -0.02 for -2%)
variables.V_s(T): Shear velocity computed at pressure P and temperature T via Mie-Gruneisen EOS (m/s)

key_equations.interpretation: This is the core equation solved by LLSVPCalculator.ComputeRequiredDeltaT using bisection. For a target dln(Vs) of -0.02 (a typical -2% LLSVP anomaly), the calculator finds the DeltaT that satisfies this relation. Because Vs decreases with increasing temperature due to softening of elastic moduli, DeltaT is positive for negative dln(Vs).

key_equations.name: Reuss (lower) bound for bulk modulus of solid-melt mixture

variables.K_{mix}: Effective bulk modulus of the mixture (GPa)
variables.K_{melt}: Bulk modulus of the melt at pressure P (GPa)
variables.K_S^{solid}: Adiabatic bulk modulus of the solid phase (GPa)

key_equations.interpretation: The Reuss bound assumes uniform stress across phases and provides the lower bound on effective bulk modulus. Used in ULVZCalculator because the melt phase interconnects at relatively low melt fractions, making the iso-stress assumption physically reasonable for partially molten rock at the CMB.

key_equations.name: Effective shear modulus of solid-melt mixture
key_equations.interpretation: Since liquids have zero shear modulus, the mixture shear modulus is simply the solid contribution scaled by the solid fraction. This linear reduction causes Vs to drop rapidly with increasing melt fraction, explaining why ULVZs show much larger Vs anomalies than Vp anomalies.

key_equations.name: Shear velocity of the solid-melt mixture
key_equations.interpretation: The factor of 1000 converts from km/s to m/s given the GPa and g/cm3 units. Since G_mix decreases linearly with melt fraction while rho_mix changes only slightly, Vs drops steeply. A 10% melt fraction typically produces >5% Vs reduction.

key_equations.name: Compressional velocity of the solid-melt mixture
key_equations.interpretation: Since the melt retains finite bulk modulus, the numerator under the square root does not vanish, so Vp is less affected than Vs by partial melting. This explains the characteristic ULVZ signature: |dVs| >> |dVp|.

key_equations.name: Third-order Birch-Murnaghan EOS for silicate melt

variables.K_0: Zero-pressure bulk modulus of the melt (GPa)

key_equations.interpretation: This EOS is used in ULVZCalculator to compute melt density at high pressure via bisection. The default melt parameters (K0 = 30 GPa, K' = 6) represent a generic silicate melt under deep mantle conditions. The melt density solution feeds into the mixture density and velocity calculations.

key_equations.name: Velocity and density contrasts between reference and anomalous assemblages

variables.V_s^{anom}: Shear velocity of anomalous (e.g., Fe-enriched) assemblage (m/s)

key_equations.interpretation: Computed by LLSVPCalculator.CompareAssemblages. For a thermochemical LLSVP, one expects dln(Vs) < 0 and dln(rho) > 0 simultaneously (slower but denser than surroundings), which is difficult to achieve with temperature alone since hot material is both slower and less dense.

physical_concepts.definition: Two continent-sized regions in the lowermost mantle (beneath Africa and the Pacific) imaged by seismic tomography as having shear velocities 2-5% lower than the global average, extending up to 1000 km above the CMB.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: These are the largest heterogeneities in the mantle and are spatially correlated with surface hotspot volcanism, large igneous provinces, and the geoid. Their sharpness and persistence suggest they are not purely thermal features (which would diffuse) but contain chemically distinct material, possibly primordial or recycled.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: LLSVPs are sometimes called 'superplumes', but they are not necessarily actively upwelling everywhere. They are broad, quasi-static structures with sharp lateral boundaries. The term 'thermochemical piles' better reflects the current understanding that both temperature and composition contribute to the anomaly.

physical_concepts.concept: Thermal versus thermochemical origin of LLSVPs
physical_concepts.definition: The debate over whether LLSVP velocity anomalies are caused solely by elevated temperature or require additional compositional heterogeneity such as iron enrichment or distinct mineralogy.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: A pure thermal anomaly (DeltaT ~ 300-800 K) can explain the velocity reduction, but it would also reduce density, making the material buoyant. Seismic observations suggest LLSVPs may be neutrally buoyant or slightly denser than surroundings, which is inconsistent with a purely thermal origin. Iron enrichment simultaneously reduces velocity and increases density, providing a more consistent explanation.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: The thermal and compositional hypotheses are not mutually exclusive. Most current models invoke both an elevated temperature (from proximity to the hot core) and a compositional anomaly (iron enrichment or subducted oceanic crust) to match the full range of seismological constraints.

physical_concepts.definition: Thin (5-40 km) patches at the core-mantle boundary exhibiting extreme seismic velocity reductions of up to -10% in Vp and -30% in Vs relative to PREM, detected by waveform modeling of core-reflected and diffracted phases.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: The extreme velocity reductions, especially the disproportionately large Vs anomaly, strongly suggest the presence of partial melt. Liquids have zero shear modulus, so even small melt fractions dramatically reduce Vs while Vp drops less because the liquid retains a finite bulk modulus.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: ULVZs are not continuous layers but localized patches, often found at the edges of LLSVPs. Not all CMB regions host ULVZs. Some ULVZs may be explained by solid iron-enriched phases (e.g., iron-rich post-perovskite) rather than partial melt, though the partial melt hypothesis remains dominant.

physical_concepts.definition: The hypothesis that temperatures at the core-mantle boundary locally exceed the solidus of mantle material, producing a small volume fraction of silicate liquid coexisting with solid bridgmanite and post-perovskite.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: The CMB temperature (~3800-4200 K) is close to or above the solidus of lower mantle material, especially if iron-enriched or volatile-bearing. Even 5-10% partial melt can explain the extreme velocity reductions observed in ULVZs. The melt is denser than overlying solid mantle (due to iron partitioning into the liquid) and therefore gravitationally stable at the CMB.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: Partial melt does not mean the entire region is liquid. A few percent melt along grain boundaries is sufficient to produce the observed velocity anomalies. The melt geometry (thin films vs. tubules vs. pockets) affects the effective elastic properties, which is why the choice of mixing model (Reuss vs. Voigt vs. Hashin-Shtrikman) matters.

physical_concepts.definition: The substitution of Fe2+ for Mg2+ in lower mantle minerals (bridgmanite, ferropericlase) reduces shear velocity, increases density, and may lower the solidus temperature.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Iron is heavier than magnesium, so Fe-bearing minerals are denser. Iron also weakens the elastic bonds (lower shear modulus) due to its larger ionic radius and different electronic configuration. In the code, comparing mpv (Mg-perovskite) with fpv (Fe-perovskite) via CompareAssemblages shows negative dln(Vs) and positive dln(rho), the signature expected for chemically anomalous LLSVPs.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: Iron enrichment alone may not explain the full magnitude of LLSVP anomalies. The amount of iron required to match -3% dVs would produce density anomalies potentially larger than observed. A combination of moderate iron enrichment and elevated temperature is likely needed.

physical_concepts.concept: Reuss bound for partially molten rock
physical_concepts.definition: An effective medium theory that assumes uniform stress (iso-stress condition) across all phases, providing the lower bound on the effective elastic modulus of a composite.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: For partial melt, the Reuss bound is appropriate because melt typically forms an interconnected network along grain boundaries at melt fractions above ~1%, meaning all phases experience the same pressure (iso-stress). This gives a lower bound on bulk modulus and, combined with G_melt = 0, produces the maximum velocity reduction for a given melt fraction.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: The Reuss bound is not the only valid model. For very low melt fractions (<1%) where melt may be isolated in pockets, the Hashin-Shtrikman upper bound or even the Voigt average might be more appropriate. The ULVZCalculator uses a Voigt-like linear mixing for shear modulus (since G_melt = 0, the choice of bound is less critical for G).

key_references.author: Garnero, E. J. and McNamara, A. K.
key_references.title: Structure and Dynamics of Earth's Lower Mantle
key_references.why_essential: Comprehensive review of LLSVPs and ULVZs that synthesizes seismological observations with geodynamic modeling, establishing the framework for interpreting these features as thermochemical structures.

key_references.author: Williams, Q. and Garnero, E. J.
key_references.title: Seismic Evidence for Partial Melt at the Base of Earth's Mantle
key_references.why_essential: Seminal paper proposing partial melt as the explanation for ULVZs, establishing the connection between extreme velocity reductions at the CMB and the presence of silicate liquid.

key_references.author: Trampert, J., Deschamps, F., Resovsky, J., and Yuen, D.
key_references.title: Probabilistic Tomography Maps Chemical Heterogeneities Throughout the Lower Mantle
key_references.why_essential: Demonstrated using probabilistic tomography that velocity and density anomalies in the lower mantle are anticorrelated, providing strong evidence for a thermochemical rather than purely thermal origin of LLSVPs.

key_references.author: McNamara, A. K. and Zhong, S.
key_references.title: Thermochemical Structures Beneath Africa and the Pacific Ocean
key_references.why_essential: Geodynamic modeling study showing that thermochemical piles can form and persist in LLSVP locations when subducted slabs sweep intrinsically dense material into two antipodal piles, matching observed LLSVP geometry.

key_references.author: Rost, S., Garnero, E. J., Williams, Q., and Manga, M.
key_references.title: Seismological Constraints on a Possible Plume Root at the Core-Mantle Boundary
key_references.why_essential: Detailed seismological study of ULVZs beneath hotspots, linking partial melt at the CMB to plume generation and demonstrating the spatial relationship between ULVZs and LLSVP margins.

key_references.author: Stixrude, L. and Lithgow-Bertelloni, C.
key_references.title: Thermodynamics of Mantle Minerals -- II. Phase Equilibria
key_references.why_essential: Provides the mineral physics database (SLB2011) used by both calculators for bridgmanite, post-perovskite, ferropericlase, and wustite endmember parameters at deep mantle conditions.

worked_examples.problem_statement: Calculate the temperature anomaly required to produce a -2% shear velocity reduction in Mg-perovskite (bridgmanite) at 120 GPa and a reference temperature of 2500 K. Interpret whether this is geophysically plausible for an LLSVP.
worked_examples.solution_approach: Use LLSVPCalculator.ComputeRequiredDeltaT with mineral = mpv (Mg-perovskite from SLB2011), P = 120 GPa, T_ref = 2500 K, and targetDlnVs = -0.02. The calculator uses bisection over DeltaT in [0, 2000 K], evaluating the Mie-Gruneisen EOS at each trial temperature to compute Vs, until ln(Vs(T_ref + DeltaT)) - ln(Vs(T_ref)) matches -0.02 within tolerance 1e-6.
worked_examples.numerical_values: The bisection converges to DeltaT in the range of approximately 300-800 K (the exact value depends on the SLB2011 parameters for mpv). At 120 GPa, Vs(2500 K) is approximately 7.0 km/s, and Vs(2500 + DeltaT) is reduced to approximately 6.86 km/s.
worked_examples.interpretation: A DeltaT of several hundred kelvin is large but not impossible for the thermal boundary layer above the CMB. However, lateral temperature variations of >500 K in the deep mantle would produce strong convective flow that would tend to dissipate the thermal anomaly. This suggests that if the LLSVP velocity anomaly exceeds what ~300-500 K can explain, a compositional contribution (iron enrichment) is needed. The test VelocityTemperatureTradeoff_RequiresDeltaT_300To600K validates this range.

worked_examples.problem_statement: Compute the velocity reductions produced by 10% partial melt in bridgmanite at CMB conditions (135 GPa, 3800 K) using default silicate melt parameters. Compare dVp and dVs.
worked_examples.solution_approach: Use ULVZCalculator.ComputeMeltMixture with solidMineral = mpv, meltFraction = 0.10, P = 135 GPa, T = 3800 K, and default MeltParams (rho0 = 3.0 g/cm3, K0 = 30 GPa, K' = 6). The calculator first solves for melt density at 135 GPa using BM3 bisection, then computes mixture K (Reuss bound), G (linear mixing with G_melt = 0), density (volume average), and velocities.
worked_examples.numerical_values: Solid bridgmanite at these conditions: Vs ~ 7.0 km/s, Vp ~ 13.5 km/s, density ~ 5.5 g/cm3. With 10% melt: G_mix = 0.9 * G_solid, and Vs_mix drops by more than 5% (test confirms dVs_pct < -5%). Vp reduction is smaller because K_mix remains substantial (melt K is ~30 GPa at ambient, much higher at 135 GPa). The ratio |dVs/dVp| > 1 is the characteristic signature of partial melt.
worked_examples.interpretation: A 10% melt fraction produces Vs reductions of 5-10%, consistent with observations of moderate ULVZs. To explain the most extreme ULVZs (dVs ~ -30%), melt fractions of 20-30% or additional iron enrichment may be required. The key diagnostic is the dVs/dVp ratio: partial melt produces |dVs| >> |dVp| because liquids retain bulk modulus but lose shear modulus entirely.

worked_examples.problem_statement: Compare the elastic properties of Mg-perovskite (mpv) and Fe-perovskite (fpv) at 120 GPa and 2500 K to quantify the effect of iron enrichment on LLSVP signatures.
worked_examples.solution_approach: Use LLSVPCalculator.CompareAssemblages with referenceMineral = mpv and anomalousMineral = fpv at P = 120 GPa, T = 2500 K. The method computes the Mie-Gruneisen EOS for both phases and returns dln(Vp), dln(Vs), and dln(rho).
worked_examples.numerical_values: Fe-perovskite has higher density (positive dln(rho)) and lower shear velocity (negative dln(Vs)) compared to Mg-perovskite. Typical values: dln(Vs) ~ -5 to -10%, dln(rho) ~ +5 to +10%, depending on the Fe content implied by the SLB2011 fpv endmember parameters. The anticorrelation between dln(Vs) and dln(rho) is the hallmark of compositional heterogeneity.
worked_examples.interpretation: The simultaneous negative dln(Vs) and positive dln(rho) from iron enrichment is exactly what seismic observations suggest for LLSVPs: they are slow but potentially dense. A purely thermal anomaly would produce negative dln(Vs) and negative dln(rho) (hot material is both slow and light). This test (FeEnrichment_ReducesVs and FeEnrichment_IncreasesDensity) confirms the thermochemical signature.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Chapter 6: Mie-Gruneisen Equation of State Solver
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: Both LLSVPCalculator and ULVZCalculator rely on MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer to compute mineral elastic properties (Vp, Vs, KS, GS, density) at arbitrary P-T conditions. The accuracy of LLSVP and ULVZ predictions depends entirely on the EOS solver.

The calculations use SLB2011 endmember parameters for bridgmanite (mpv, fpv), periclase (pe), and wustite (wu). The quality of the mineral database directly determines the reliability of velocity and density predictions at deep mantle conditions.

The ULVZCalculator employs the Reuss lower bound for bulk modulus mixing and a linear (Voigt-like) model for shear modulus mixing. Understanding the theoretical basis and validity regimes of these mixing models is essential for interpreting ULVZ results.

The post-perovskite transition occurs at similar P-T conditions to LLSVPs and ULVZs. Post-perovskite may coexist with partial melt in ULVZs, and lateral variations in the pPv transition depth interact with LLSVP thermal and compositional anomalies.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Chapter 15: PREM and Radial Earth Models
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: LLSVP and ULVZ anomalies are measured relative to the PREM reference model. Understanding what constitutes 'normal' lower mantle properties (from PREM) is necessary to quantify the anomalies that LLSVPCalculator and ULVZCalculator aim to explain.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Chapter 4: Elastic Moduli and Seismic Velocities
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: The relationships between elastic moduli (K, G), density, and seismic velocities (Vp, Vs) are fundamental to interpreting both LLSVP velocity anomalies and ULVZ partial melt signatures.

Real LLSVPs and ULVZs involve multi-mineral assemblages (bridgmanite + ferropericlase + CaSiO3-perovskite), not single endmembers. The rock composition framework enables more realistic modeling of deep mantle heterogeneity.

computational_methods:
The LLSVPCalculator uses a bisection algorithm to find the temperature anomaly DeltaT that produces a target dln(Vs). The search interval is [0, 2000] K with convergence tolerance of 1e-6 on dln(Vs), limited to 100 iterations. At each iteration, the Mie-Gruneisen EOS is solved at (P, T_ref + DeltaT_trial) to obtain Vs, and the logarithmic velocity difference is compared to the target. The CompareAssemblages method simply evaluates the EOS for two different mineral endmembers at identical P-T and computes logarithmic ratios.

The ULVZCalculator involves two nested numerical procedures. First, the melt density at the specified pressure is found by bisection on the BM3 equation P(rho) = P_target, searching over [rho0, 3*rho0] with 200 iterations and tolerance 1e-6 GPa. Second, the melt bulk modulus at the solved density is computed analytically from the BM3 derivatives. The solid-melt mixture properties are then computed using closed-form effective medium expressions: Reuss bound for K, linear mixing for G (with G_melt = 0), and volume-weighted arithmetic average for density. Velocities follow directly from Vp = sqrt((K + 4G/3)/rho) and Vs = sqrt(G/rho). The unit conversion factor of 1000 accounts for GPa and g/cm3 units yielding m/s.

Both calculators have O(N_iter * C_EOS) complexity per evaluation, where N_iter is the number of bisection iterations (~50-100) and C_EOS is the cost of a single Mie-Gruneisen EOS evaluation. Performance is adequate for interactive use in the desktop UI (sub-second response times).
open_questions: Are LLSVPs thermochemical piles of primordial material, accumulations of subducted oceanic crust, or a combination of both? The iron content, major element composition, and volatile budget remain poorly constrained.

What is the melt composition and iron partitioning between solid and liquid phases at CMB conditions? The effective MeltParams (K0, K', rho0) for iron-rich silicate melts at 135 GPa are highly uncertain.
Do all ULVZs require partial melt, or can some be explained by solid iron-rich phases such as (Mg,Fe)O with very high iron content or iron-rich post-perovskite?

How do LLSVPs interact with mantle convection over geological time? Are they stable features that have persisted since Earth's formation, or are they dynamically maintained by the balance of slab sweeping and buoyancy forces?

What is the sharpness and nature of LLSVP boundaries? Seismic observations suggest sharp lateral boundaries (~50 km), but it is unclear whether this reflects a chemical boundary, a phase transition, or a thermal boundary layer.

How does the spin transition in iron-bearing minerals (ferropericlase, bridgmanite) affect velocity anomalies in LLSVPs? The high-spin to low-spin crossover in Fe2+ changes elastic properties and density in ways not yet fully incorporated into LLSVP models.
What role do hydrogen and other volatiles play in lowering the solidus at the CMB and promoting partial melt in ULVZs?
exercise_suggestions: Use LLSVPCalculator.ComputeRequiredDeltaT to determine the DeltaT needed for dln(Vs) values of -1%, -2%, -3%, and -5% in Mg-perovskite at 120 GPa. Plot DeltaT vs dln(Vs) and discuss the linearity of the relationship.

Compare the results of CompareAssemblages for mpv vs fpv at pressures of 80, 100, 120, and 135 GPa. How do dln(Vs), dln(Vp), and dln(rho) change with pressure? Discuss implications for depth-dependent LLSVP signatures.

Using ULVZCalculator, compute dVp and dVs for melt fractions of 0%, 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, and 30% at CMB conditions. Plot both velocity reductions versus melt fraction and determine what melt fraction is needed to match a -10% Vp and -30% Vs anomaly.

Modify the MeltParams to represent an iron-rich silicate melt (higher rho0 = 4.0 g/cm3, K0 = 40 GPa) and recompute the ULVZ velocity reductions at 10% melt fraction. Compare with the default melt and discuss how melt composition affects the ULVZ signature.

For a thermochemical LLSVP model, compute the combined effect of +300 K temperature anomaly and Fe-enrichment (using fpv instead of mpv) at 120 GPa. Does the combined dln(Vs) match observations better than either effect alone?

Compute Vs for periclase (pe) and wustite (wu) at 135 GPa and various temperatures. At what Fe/(Fe+Mg) ratio (interpolating between pe and wu properties) does the velocity reduction match typical ULVZ observations?

Investigate the sensitivity of ULVZCalculator results to the melt EOS parameters. Vary K0 from 20 to 50 GPa and K' from 4 to 8, computing dVs for 10% melt each time. Which parameter has the larger effect on the predicted velocity reduction?

Using ComputeAtCMB, compute bridgmanite properties at P = 135 GPa for temperatures from 3000 K to 4500 K in 200 K steps. Determine the temperature-velocity gradient (dVs/dT) at CMB conditions and compare it with upper mantle values.

figure_suggestions.description: DeltaT vs dln(Vs) curve for Mg-perovskite at 120 GPa
figure_suggestions.key_features: Show the temperature anomaly needed for various velocity reductions. Highlight the range corresponding to observed LLSVP anomalies (-2 to -5%) and indicate the geophysically plausible DeltaT range (~300-800 K). Mark the point where purely thermal explanation becomes problematic.

figure_suggestions.description: Velocity reduction vs melt fraction for ULVZ at CMB conditions
figure_suggestions.key_features: Plot both dVp and dVs on the same axes. Show that dVs drops much faster than dVp. Add horizontal lines indicating typical ULVZ observations (-10% Vp, -30% Vs). The divergence between the two curves demonstrates why |dVs/dVp| > 1 is diagnostic of partial melt.

figure_suggestions.description: Compositional vs thermal velocity anomaly diagram
figure_suggestions.key_features: Plot vectors showing the effect of temperature increase (negative dln(Vs), negative dln(rho)) and iron enrichment (negative dln(Vs), positive dln(rho)). Show how combining both effects can produce the observed LLSVP signature (slow, neutrally buoyant to dense). Label the quadrants: thermal only (lower-left), compositional only (lower-right), combined (between).

figure_suggestions.description: Cross-section schematic of LLSVP and ULVZ relationship at the CMB
figure_suggestions.key_features: Show a large LLSVP thermochemical pile extending ~1000 km above the CMB with sharp lateral boundaries. At the LLSVP margins, show thin ULVZ patches (5-40 km thick) with partial melt. Indicate hot upwelling plumes originating from the LLSVP edges. Label velocity anomalies in each region.

figure_suggestions.description: Comparison of Mg-perovskite vs Fe-perovskite properties at lower mantle pressures
figure_suggestions.key_features: Dual y-axis plot showing Vs and density for both mpv and fpv from 80 to 135 GPa at 2500 K. Highlight the velocity deficit and density excess of fpv relative to mpv. Annotate the CMB pressure (~135 GPa) and typical LLSVP pressure range.

historical_context:
The existence of large-scale heterogeneity in the deep mantle was first suggested by early global seismic tomography studies in the 1980s, notably by Dziewonski (1984) and Woodhouse and Dziewonski (1984), who revealed the degree-2 pattern of low shear velocities beneath Africa and the Pacific. The term 'superplume' was initially used, implying active upwelling, but subsequent studies showed these are broad, quasi-static features better described as thermochemical piles. The thermochemical interpretation gained momentum with the probabilistic tomography work of Trampert et al. (2004), which demonstrated anticorrelated velocity and density anomalies inconsistent with purely thermal origins.

ULVZs were first identified by Garnero and Helmberger (1995, 1996) through detailed waveform modeling of core-diffracted phases (SPdKS and ScS), revealing thin zones of extreme velocity reduction at the CMB. Williams and Garnero (1996) proposed partial melt as the explanation, connecting the extreme Vs reduction to the zero shear modulus of liquid. The spatial correlation between ULVZs and hotspot locations, documented by Williams et al. (1998) and Rost et al. (2005), suggested a genetic link between CMB partial melt and mantle plume generation.

The connection between LLSVPs and ULVZs was solidified by geodynamic modeling, particularly McNamara and Zhong (2005), who showed that subducting slabs can sweep dense material into two thermochemical piles whose geometry matches the observed LLSVPs. The iron enrichment hypothesis was supported by mineral physics experiments showing that Fe-bearing perovskite has lower velocity and higher density than Mg-perovskite at deep mantle conditions. Recent advances in large-scale seismic imaging, including full-waveform inversion and array processing, continue to refine the geometry of LLSVPs and the distribution of ULVZs, while ab initio calculations and laser-heated diamond anvil cell experiments constrain the elastic properties and melting behavior of relevant phases at CMB conditions.
